title: Making a foam book cradle for reading and display
subtitle: A simple wedge support that holds a book open without stressing the spine
excerpt: A book cradle holds a bound volume open at a comfortable reading angle while distributing the weight of the covers across a wide surface rather than concentrating stress at the spine joints. The version here uses a single piece of high-density foam, cut and covered in a few minutes.
sourceNotes: Standard bookbinding conservation practice. Foam book cradles are recommended by the British Library and the Library of Congress for handling rare or damaged bound volumes; the dimensions given here are the home-studio adaptation used for display and working reading stands.

glossaryTerms.definition: A V-shaped or wedge support designed to hold a bound book open at a set angle while distributing the load away from the spine joints.

attrs.title: Cutting tools
attrs.body: Use sharp blades only; change when they dull. Cut on a self-healing mat against a metal ruler, non-cutting hand well back.

content.text: is the correct way to read a heavy or fragile bound volume. When you hold a book open in your hands or prop it flat on a table, the weight of the open covers stresses the spine joints repeatedly, joints that were never designed to take cantilever loads. A cradle spreads the weight across the board faces and holds the book at a reading angle of roughly 120 degrees, which is comfortable for the spine and comfortable for the reader.

items.name: High-density polyethylene foam (Ethafoam or equivalent), 20 to 30 mm thick
items.qty: one piece at least 30 cm x 20 cm
items.substitutions: A piece of dense upholstery foam works; avoid soft open-cell foam, which compresses too easily

items.name: Cotton or linen cover cloth, sufficient to wrap the cradle
items.substitutions: A clean T-shirt offcut, any non-abrasive cloth

content.text: Cut two rectangular foam blocks, each 20 cm wide, 15 cm deep, and 25 mm thick. These form the two ramps of the cradle.
On each block, mark a diagonal line from the top front edge to the bottom back edge. Cut along this line with the craft knife. The result is a wedge, thin at the spine end, thicker at the board end.
Place the two wedges together, sloped sides facing each other, thin edges at the centre. The combined assembly is V-shaped: the book spine sits in the valley, the boards rest on the outer slopes.
Check that the angle is comfortable for reading. The angle of each wedge slope determines the opening angle of the book; a 60-degree slope on each side gives a reading angle of about 120 degrees.

Bare foam can scratch a delicate spine and picks up dust. Wrap each wedge in cloth before joining them. Cut the cloth large enough to wrap around all sides and tuck under the base. Glue with a thin coat of PVA; fold the cloth as you would wrap a parcel, with mitre-cut corners.
Leave the two halves unglued so the cradle width can be adjusted for books of different spine widths. Rest a small batten of wood across the base to keep the two halves parallel.

Open the book to the desired page and place it in the cradle with the spine in the valley. Spread the two halves apart slightly to match the spine width, the boards should rest level on the slopes without the spine hanging free. Do not force the book open past its natural resistance; if the book does not open to 120 degrees without strain, use a narrower angle cradle or fewer pages at a time.

A single-piece cradle cut from one thick block of foam with a V-groove routed or cut into the centre works for display. The adjustable two-piece version described here is more practical for working use.
